citation: Lessiter, J., Freeman, J., Keogh, E., & Davidoff, J. (2001). A cross-media presence questionnaire: The ITC-Sense of Presence Inventory. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 10(3), 282-297.
intended_use: Cross-media (VR, television, cinema, desktop displays); designed to be media-agnostic

sense_of_physical_space.label: Sense of Physical Space (SPS)
sense_of_physical_space.description: The degree to which participants feel physically present within the mediated environment — spatial awareness, sense of being located in the virtual world.
example_items: I had a strong sense of being in the environment shown.
I felt I was visiting the places shown in the presentation.
I felt I could reach out and touch things in the presentation.
I felt my body was 'in' the environment shown.
The sense of 'being there' was strong.
sense_of_physical_space.scoring: Mean of all SPS items (higher = more physical presence)

engagement.label: Engagement (ENG)
engagement.description: The degree to which participants experienced involvement, enjoyment, and found the experience interesting and compelling.
example_items: I was interested in what was going on in the presentation.
I was involved in the presentation.

naturalness.label: Naturalness (NAT)
naturalness.description: The degree to which the experience felt natural, coherent, and consistent with real-world expectations — ecological validity of the virtual environment.
example_items: The way things moved in the presentation was consistent with how I would expect them to move in real life.
The objects in the presentation looked like real objects.
The sounds seemed to come from the right places.

negative_effects.label: Negative Effects (NE)
negative_effects.description: The degree to which participants experienced discomfort, disorientation, nausea, or other negative symptoms — analogous to simulator/cybersickness.
negative_effects.scoring: Mean of all NE items; higher scores indicate MORE negative effects (reverse interpretation)
negative_effects.note: This subscale is unique to ITC-SOPI among canonical presence scales; captures adverse reactions

scoring_instructions.reverse_score_procedure: Some items within subscales are reverse-keyed; consult original scoring key
scoring_instructions.total_score: No single total score recommended; four subscale scores reported independently
scoring_instructions.interpretation: Higher SPS/ENG/NAT = more presence/engagement/naturalness; higher NE = more adverse effects

psychometric_properties.internal_consistency: Cronbach alpha: SPS=0.90, ENG=0.87, NAT=0.82, NE=0.88 (original study)
psychometric_properties.factor_structure: 4-factor solution from factor analysis of 44-item pool
psychometric_properties.validity: Validated across TV, cinema, and VR conditions in original study

key_differentiators: Only canonical presence scale with a dedicated Negative Effects subscale
Designed for cross-media use — not VR-specific
Does NOT include items about specific system hardware or interface quality
Widely used in ISPR conference community (most popular there per Rosakranse & Oh, 2014)
theoretical_grounding: Lessiter et al. factor analysis; influenced by IJsselsteijn et al. (2000) presence determinants model
